Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place, but I tried to make it work with what I had. I'd usually study late at night after my shifts or early in the morning before classes. I dedicated about 27 hours a week, which sounds like a lot, but I had to spread it thin across nine online courses. I'd write out notes by hand since that's how I learn best, and I'd organize my materials in folders on my laptop to access offline when I needed to.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. When deadlines were coming up, I'd push harder and study more intensively. But other weeks, especially when work got busy or I had family stuff to deal with, I'd fall behind and have to catch up later. I tried to stick to a schedule, but life with limited resources means you have to be flexible and adapt constantly.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew an assignment was coming due or when I needed to check announcements. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone that reminded me to check something. Other times, my peer study group would message me saying there was new material posted, and that would prompt me to go look. I wasn't logging in just to browse—it was always purposeful because my time and data were limited.
Q4: Honestly, the LMS never really became a natural habit for me. I'd use it when I absolutely needed to, but it felt more like a tool I accessed out of necessity rather than something integrated into my daily routine. The peer support group I was part of helped because we'd remind each other to check for updates. That external accountability made me more consistent than I would have been on my own.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were the most useful for me since I'm a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and articles so I could study them offline later. The discussion forums were also helpful when I could connect with peers and get different perspectives on confusing topics. Those interactions made me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q6: I often skipped the video content or only watched parts of it because it took too long and used up my data. With over 119 minutes of video to watch, I had to be selective about what was essential. Some of the optional supplementary materials I'd delay until I had more time, which often meant I never got to them. I prioritized what was directly tied to assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I was struggling with or if it counted toward my grade. When I could see the connection between what I was doing and passing the course, I was all in. But if something felt like busy work or wasn't clearly connected to the outcomes, I'd skip it because I simply didn't have time to waste.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required activities got my attention first because those affected my grade directly. With nine courses and limited time, I had to be strategic. Optional stuff might have been valuable, but when you're already stretched thin, you have to make hard choices about where to invest your energy.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download materials and read them offline, take handwritten notes, and review them on paper. I also used free resources I found online—YouTube videos, open textbooks, anything I could access without paying. My peer group would share notes and summaries too, which was incredibly helpful and wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd have my notes spread out on my bed, reviewing concepts for hours, or I'd be discussing problems with my study group over WhatsApp. Sometimes I'd reread the same downloaded PDF multiple times to really understand it. All that effort was invisible to the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was my main strategy actually. I'd access materials once, download everything I could, and then study offline for days or even weeks. Data costs money, so I couldn't afford to keep streaming or reloading pages. Once I had the files, I'd work through them at my own pace without needing to log back in.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when my anxiety spiked around exam time. My attendance dropped to around 61% partly because I was dealing with a lot of stress and sometimes just couldn't make myself show up. Financial pressures and family responsibilities also pulled my attention away from coursework. Those were the times when even logging in felt overwhelming.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion about course material made it worse because I'd feel anxious about not understanding, which made me avoid it more. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to choose between studying and other obligations, and sometimes studying lost.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in, technically looking at content, but my mind was elsewhere—worrying about bills or just too tired to focus. Those sessions felt hollow. I was going through the motions but not actually learning.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation more than I should have, even though I knew it was critical. The anxiety around it was so intense that avoiding it felt easier in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd study tomorrow, but then tomorrow would bring new problems. It's a cycle I'm still working on breaking—my resilience helps me bounce back, but the avoidance still happens.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for using the LMS. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached and sometimes pull all-nighters to get things submitted. My assignment completion was pretty high at 87.9% because I'd push through no matter what when something was due. But that reactive pattern meant I wasn't engaging consistently throughout the course.
Q17: Feedback definitely mattered to me. When I got positive comments, it motivated me to keep pushing even when things were hard. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse initially, but I'd use it to improve. The peer support I received helped me process feedback better—talking it through with others made it feel less personal and more constructive.
Q18: The course structure was challenging because having nine online courses meant everything overlapped and competed for my attention. When modules were clearly laid out with explicit expectations, I could manage better. But when things were vague or scattered, I'd waste time figuring out what I was supposed to do instead of actually learning.
Q19: Clear deadlines and organized modules made engagement easier. What made it harder was when there was too much content without guidance on what to prioritize. Also, required synchronous sessions were tough for me because of my work schedule and inconsistent internet. More flexibility would have helped someone in my situation engage more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying hard but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're reviewing materials repeatedly because they don't understand, or they're anxious and keep checking things without actually completing assignments. It could also be someone dealing with perfectionism or fear of failure—lots of activity but paralyzed when it comes to submitting.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I performed better on assignments than on exams, and a lot of my real learning happened offline where the LMS couldn't track it. Someone might have strong study skills and external resources that let them learn effectively without heavy platform use. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and whether you submitted things on time. But they miss the quality of your engagement, the learning that happens offline, and the emotional context behind your behavior. They can't see that I was up at 2 AM handwriting notes from downloaded PDFs, or that I was discussing concepts with peers over text messages.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas to real life, and pushing through even when it's hard. For me, engagement also means reaching out to peers when I'm stuck and not giving up even when my anxiety tells me to. It's mental and emotional investment, not just clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the late-night study sessions, the peer support that kept me going, or the anxiety I pushed through every time I opened an assignment. It wouldn't show my resilience—how I kept coming back even after failing the exam. The numbers tell one story, but they miss the human struggle behind them.
